claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 2 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 8 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about three of my kids - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. 

Alex is 10 years old, Blake is 19, and Charlie is 2.

Should we go through them one at a time?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! Yes, it's helpful to go through each child one at a time to ensure we get accurate assessments for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me start with **Alex (10 years old)** since they're under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10 years old):**

Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all? 

(This is just a standard check - most 10-year-olds aren't in paid employment, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 10!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's what I expected. Let me continue with Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10 years old):**

Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Again, this is unlikely for a 10-year-old, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10 years old):**

Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?

(This is very unlikely for a 10-year-old, but it's part of the standard checks.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to ask about you (the claimant):**

Do you meet the UK residency requirements? You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. You normally live in the UK
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with pre-settled status
8. You're a Crown servant living abroad

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That satisfies the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10 years old):**

Are you responsible for Alex? You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. You live with Alex
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10 years old):**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex (for example, another parent or guardian)?

- If no one else is claiming, we can continue
- If someone else is claiming but you've agreed they'll transfer it to you, that's fine too
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

What's the situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10 years old):**

I need to check Alex's living situation. Which of the following applies to Alex?

1. **In hospital** - currently staying in hospital
2. **In care** - placed in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - in a foster care arrangement
4. **Normal living** - living at home with you normally

Which situation describes Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but they're not there full-time - they come home to stay with me regularly.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me check the care situation for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10 years old):**

This is an important question about Alex's care arrangement. You mentioned Alex has been in care for a couple of months. 

**You can still claim Child Benefit if:**
- Alex has been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR
- Alex spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you (this could be 2 consecutive nights per week, or 7+ days in a row)

You said Alex comes home regularly. Does Alex spend at least 24 hours per week with you at home? For example, does Alex come home for weekends or spend significant time with you each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Let me think... Alex has been in care for about 7 weeks now, so that's still within the 8-week period you mentioned.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex has been in care for 7 weeks, which is within the 8-week period, that satisfies the requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10 years old):**

Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance while they're in care?

Generally, you cannot claim Child Benefit if the council is paying for the child's care costs.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me record the outcome for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Great news for Alex (10 years old)! ✓ ELIGIBLE**

Alex meets all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements.

---

Now let's assess **Charlie (2 years old)**. Since Charlie is also under 16, let me start with the same checks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (2 years old):**

Charlie is under 16, so let me ask the standard questions:

1. Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

(I assume not, given Charlie's age, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't work - they're only 2!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (2 years old):**

Is Charlie receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (2 years old):**

Is Charlie in any type of apprenticeship? (Obviously not at age 2, but confirming for the record.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie isn't in an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: We've already confirmed you live in the UK (Manchester), so that satisfies the residency requirement for Charlie as well.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (2 years old):**

Does Charlie live with you at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't live with me. I'm not currently able to contribute financially to their upkeep either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Thank you for being honest about that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (2 years old): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you're not eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie because you need to be responsible for the child. This means either:
- Living with them, OR
- Paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.)

Since Charlie doesn't live with you and you're not currently able to contribute financially to their upkeep, you don't meet the responsibility requirement.

---

Now let's assess **Blake (19 years old)**. Since Blake is between 16-19, there are additional education/training requirements to check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old):**

Is Blake currently:
1. **In education or training** (approved course)
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** of these

Which applies to Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake isn't in education or training, and they didn't recently leave school either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. Let me record that outcome for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you're not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because children aged 16-19 must be in approved education or training to qualify. Since Blake is not currently in education or training, and hasn't recently left (within the last 20 weeks), Blake doesn't meet the qualifying criteria.

---

## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility:

Let me now provide you with the complete assessment for all three children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## **Final Assessment Summary**

### ✓ **Alex (10 years old) - ELIGIBLE**
You can claim Child Benefit for Alex. Even though Alex has been in care for 7 weeks, this is within the 8-week period allowed, and the council isn't paying for their care.

### ✗ **Blake (19 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Blake doesn't qualify because 16-19 year olds must be in approved education or training, which Blake is not.

### ✗ **Charlie (2 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
You cannot claim for Charlie because you don't meet the responsibility requirement (Charlie doesn't live with you and you're not financially supporting them).

---

## **Next Steps for Alex:**

Since you're eligible to claim for Alex, here's what to do:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** if you or your partner earns over £60,000
5. **Payments are made every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about the assessment or the application process?

reasonings: Alex (10 years old) is ELIGIBLE. Alex is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester). You are responsible for Alex as they live with you at home. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in care for 7 weeks, this is within the 8-week allowable period, and the local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (19 years old) is NOT ELIGIBLE. Blake is 19 years old, which falls in the 16-19 age category. For children aged 16-19 to qualify for Child Benefit, they must be in approved education or training, or have recently left education (within 20 weeks). Blake is not currently in education or training and did not recently leave education. Therefore, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Charlie (2 years old) is NOT ELIGIBLE. Although Charlie is under 16 years old (age 2), you are not responsible for Charlie under Child Benefit rules. To be considered responsible, you must either live with the child OR contribute financially to their upkeep (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount). Charlie does not live with you, and you confirmed you are not currently able to contribute financially to their upkeep. Without meeting the responsibility requirement, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have three children: Alex (10), Blake (19), and Charlie (2). You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex only. For Blake, the claim fails because 16-19 year olds must be in approved education or training to qualify. For Charlie, the claim fails because you don't meet the responsibility requirement (not living with or financially supporting Charlie). You meet all other general requirements including UK residency (living in Manchester).

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (10 years old) is ELIGIBLE. Alex is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester). You are responsible for Alex as they live with you at home. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in care for 7 weeks, this is within the 8-week allowable period, and the local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (19 years old) is NOT ELIGIBLE. Blake is 19 years old, which falls in the 16-19 age category. For children aged 16-19 to qualify for Child Benefit, they must be in approved education or training, or have recently left education (within 20 weeks). Blake is not currently in education or training and did not recently leave education. Therefore, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Charlie (2 years old) is NOT ELIGIBLE. Although Charlie is under 16 years old (age 2), you are not responsible for Charlie under Child Benefit rules. To be considered responsible, you must either live with the child OR contribute financially to their upkeep (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount). Charlie does not live with you, and you confirmed you are not currently able to contribute financially to their upkeep. Without meeting the responsibility requirement, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: You have three children: Alex (10), Blake (19), and Charlie (2). You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex only. For Blake, the claim fails because 16-19 year olds must be in approved education or training to qualify. For Charlie, the claim fails because you don't meet the responsibility requirement (not living with or financially supporting Charlie). You meet all other general requirements including UK residency (living in Manchester).
